explanation_md: `show(42)` and `show(3.14)` use the primary template and print with the `generic:` prefix. `show(true)` uses the full specialization for `bool`, which prints `yes` without the prefix. The compiler always picks the most specific match.

prompt_md: Can you write a partial template specialization for a function template (e.g., `template <typename T> void f<T*>(T* p)`)?

options.a: Yes — partial specialization works the same way for function and class templates
options.b: No — function templates only support full specialization; use overloading for partial matching
options.c: Yes, but only if the function is a member of a class
options.d: No — function templates cannot be specialized at all

explanation_md: C++ does not allow partial specialization of function templates. Only class (and variable) templates support partial specialization. For functions, use overloading to handle different patterns of types — a separate `template <typename T> void f(T* p)` overload matches all pointers and works alongside the original template.

`N` is a non-type template parameter bound at compile time. `Fixed<int, 5>` has `N = 5` baked in; `size()` returns 5. `Fixed<double, 3>` has `N = 3`; `size()` returns 3. Each instantiation is a distinct class with its own compile-time constant.

options.a: The primary template is wrong and should also use a pointer member
options.b: The pointer specialization stores the pointer itself (`T* ptr_`) instead of copying the value; changing `x` changes what `get()` returns
options.c: `get()` should return `T*` not `T` to avoid the copy overhead
options.d: The specialization is missing a `const` qualifier on `ptr_`

explanation_md: The pointer specialization stores the raw pointer, so `get()` always dereferences the current pointed-to value. When `x` changes to 99, `*ptr_` is 99. The fix is to store `T copy_` instead: `explicit Store(T* p) : copy_{*p} {}` and `T get() const { return copy_; }`. This way the value is captured at construction time and is independent of what happens to `x` later.

prompt_md: A full class template specialization `template <> class Wrapper<bool> { ... };` is different from the primary `template <typename T> class Wrapper`. Which of the following is true?

options.a: The specialization must have the same member variables and functions as the primary
options.b: The specialization is an entirely separate class — it can have completely different members, constructors, and functions
options.c: The specialization automatically inherits all members of the primary and can only add new ones
options.d: The specialization can only override member functions, not add new ones

explanation_md: A full class template specialization is compiled as a completely independent class definition. It shares no members with the primary template unless you explicitly define them. You can add different data members, different constructors, and different functions — or even remove functions that the primary has. This is what makes specialization powerful for types like `bool` that need fundamentally different storage or behavior.
